First, they are likely to accelerate the rate of job turnover and disruption —– affecting more workers, including more highly skilled workers, and making the adjustment process for society more difficult than what we were used to from prior technological advances...Second, although technological progress means that society may produce more wealth overall, there is a risk that the general-purpose nature of LLMs may lead to progress that is biased against labor, meaning that the share of that wealth that goes to labor may decline...Third, if future LLMs and robots advance to the point where they can perform virtually all the work tasks, they would disrupt labor markets more fundamentally: if machines can do workers’ jobs, wages would fall would disrupt labor markets more fundamentally: if machines can do workers’ jobs, wages would fall to machines’ user cost (Korinek and Juelfs, 2023). This would pose fundamental challenges for labor markets and income distribution (Korinek, 2023).

label: Effects on inequality
keywords: Effects on inequality LLMs could potentially worsen socioeconomic inequalities (Capraro et al., 2023). Effects on inequal- ity are closely linked to the effects of LLMs on workers but ultimately depend on how the fruits of technological progress are distributed...First, if the role and compensation of capital rise and the role and compensation of labor decline in an LLM-powered economy, inequality may go up because work is the main source of income for the majority of people...Second, the large fixed cost of training cutting-edge LLMs and the network effects involved imply that the market for the most advanced LLMs tends towards a natural monopoly structure in which only one or a small number of players will be successful, a phenomenon that has been termed ‘algorithmic monoculture’ in the literature (Kleinberg and Raghavan, 2021; Bommasani et al., 2022). As a result, LLM developers may amass significant market power. This might result in reduced social welfare, and lead to LLM-providers extracting monopoly rents from their customers (Kleinberg and Raghavan, 2021; Jagadeesan et al., 2023)...Third, as LLMs are becoming more powerful, who has access and who hasn’t is becoming a more and more important question. For example, automated coding tools have been shown to produce significant productivity gains, e.g. > 50% in some cases (Peng et al., 2023). Individuals who don’t have access —– whether it is for financial reasons, for reasons of education, because of corporate or governm

label: Global economic development
keywords: Global economic development Many of the themes and challenges that we discussed above come together when analyzing the socioeconomic effects on developing countries. The workforce of developing countries may suffer from a retrenchment of outsourcing as many simple cognitive tasks that used to be performed in developing countries — for example, in call centers –— can be automated with LLMs. This may adversely affect the economies of the poor countries (Georgieva, 2024).

label: Corporate power may impeded effective governance
keywords: Corporate power may impeded effective governance The increasing power and influence of large corporations may make effective governance difficult. There exists a power asymmetry between corporate entities profiting from LLMs and other social groups (e.g. civil society). State-of-the-art LLMs are developed by or in partnership with, some of the world’s largest private tech companies...This poses a risk of governance protocols related to LLMs becoming excessively favorable to tech companies, potentially leading to regulatory capture at the cost of the interests of other societal groups, particularly marginalized communities who have historically been disproportionately affected by poorly designed AI technologies (Reventlow, 2021).

label: Jailbreaks and prompt injections threaten security of LLMs
keywords: Jailbreaks and prompt injections threaten security of LLMs LLMs are not adversarially robust and are vulnerable to security failures such as jailbreaks and prompt-injection attacks. While a number of jailbreak attacks have been proposed in the literature, the lack of standardized evaluation makes it difficult to compare them. We also do not have efficient white-box methods to evaluate adver- sarial robustness. Multi-modal LLMs may further allow novel types of jailbreaks via additional modalities. Finally, the lack of robust privilege levels within the LLM input means that jailbreaking and prompt-injection attacks may be particularly hard to eliminate altogether.

label: “Model psychology” attacks
keywords: “Model psychology” attacks LLMs are vulnerable to “psychological” tricks (Li et al., 2023e; Shen et al., 2023), which can be exploited by attackers. Examples include instructing the model to behave like a specific persona (Shah et al., 2023; Andreas, 2022), or employing various “social engineering” tricks crafted by humans (Wei et al., 2023c) or other LLMs (Perez et al., 2022b; Casper et al., 2023c)

label: Adversarial optimization
keywords: Adversarial optimization Jailbreak attacks can be discovered by performing manual or auto- mated adversarial optimization against a proxy objective that is noisily correlated with the success of a jailbreak. These are mostly gradient-based attacks (Zou et al., 2023b; Shin et al., 2020) as described in the previous two challenges, but gradient-free methods also exist (Prasad et al., 2022; Deng et al., 2022; Lapid et al., 2023)

label: Vulnerability to poisoning and backdoors
keywords: Vulnerability to poisoning and backdoors The previous section explored jailbreaks and other forms of adversarial prompts as ways to elicit harmful capabilities acquired during pretraining. These methods make no assumptions about the training data. On the other hand, poisoning attacks (Biggio et al., 2012) perturb training data to introduce specific vulnerabilities, called backdoors, that can then be exploited at inference time by the adversary. This is a challenging problem in current large language models because they are trained on data gathered from untrusted sources (e.g. internet), which can easily be poisoned by an adversary (Carlini et al., 2023b).

label: Value-related risks in LLMs
keywords: Value-related risks in LLMs As the general capabilities of LLM-empowered systems improve, the negative consequences and risks induced by these systems also get increasingly alarming accordingly, especially in high-stakes areas [28, 146]. Although they may not be intentionally introduced, severe problematic issues related to human values can be raised. Specifically, even before language models become extremely large, pre-trained language models have already exhibited a certain degree of value judgments. For example, Schramowski et al. [171] reveal the existence of the moral direction with the sentence embeddings of moral questions. However, the distribution of the pre-training corpora may not match exactly with that of the human society [56] and pieces of knowledge are not guaranteed to be equally learned. As a result, value mismatches may occur.

label: Tool misuse / unsafe tool invocation
keywords: Tool misuse / unsafe tool invocation An agentic system invokes external tools or APIs in unintended or unsafe ways, translating compromised reasoning into consequential external actions

label: Memory poisoning
keywords: Memory poisoning Adversarial manipulation of an agent's persistent memory store such that corrupted entries bias later retrieval, planning, and action

label: Excessive agency
keywords: Excessive agency An autonomous agent takes actions exceeding the scope, permissions, or intent the deployer authorized, due to over-broad autonomy or goal generalization

label: Planning / reasoning-chain hijacking
keywords: Planning / reasoning-chain hijacking Adversarial inputs redirect an agent's multi-step plan toward attacker-chosen subgoals while preserving surface plausibility of intermediate steps

label: Indirect prompt injection
keywords: Indirect prompt injection Untrusted data ingested by an agent (web pages, documents, tool outputs) contains hidden instructions that the agent executes as if issued by the user, without any direct adversarial prompt

label: Shutdown resistance / corrigibility failure
keywords: Shutdown resistance / corrigibility failure An autonomous agent actively resists, evades, or sabotages shutdown or correction mechanisms rather than complying, undermining human control

label: Action-space safety misalignment
keywords: Action-space safety misalignment An embodied LLM verbally refuses a harmful request while still executing the corresponding physical action, due to misalignment between linguistic and action output spaces

label: Embodied jailbreak to physical harm
keywords: Embodied jailbreak to physical harm Jailbreak of an embodied LLM translates compromised reasoning into irreversible real-world physical actions (e.g., manipulation causing injury or damage)

label: Conceptual deception inducing unsafe action
keywords: Conceptual deception inducing unsafe action Reframing a harmful physical task in benign conceptual terms induces an embodied agent to perform unrecognized harmful behavior

label: Physical-world backdoor in robotic manipulation
keywords: Physical-world backdoor in robotic manipulation A backdoor trigger embedded in training data or prompt context causes a vision-language-action robot to execute dangerous or unintended physical actions while behaving normally otherwise

label: Secret collusion via steganography
keywords: Secret collusion via steganography Agents establish covert communication channels (e.g., steganographic signaling) enabling coordination that evades human oversight, even at an oversight layer

label: Emergent agency at collective level
keywords: Emergent agency at collective level Goals or capabilities arising at the level of an agent collective that were not present in, or intended by, any individual agent

label: Selection-pressure-driven harmful adaptation
keywords: Selection-pressure-driven harmful adaptation Competitive or optimization pressures across interacting agents accelerate adaptation toward harmful equilibria or undesired behaviors

label: Destabilising feedback dynamics
keywords: Destabilising feedback dynamics Interactions among adaptive agents produce destabilising feedback loops (e.g., flash-crash-like cascades) at the system level

label: Specification and system design failures
keywords: Specification and system design failures MAS failures originating from design decisions: task misinterpretation, ambiguous or disobeyed role/task specification, and poor workflow decomposition (≈41.8% of observed failures)

label: Inter-agent misalignment
keywords: Inter-agent misalignment MAS failures from breakdown in information flow and coordination between agents: communication loss, context collapse, conflicting objectives (≈36.9% of observed failures)

label: Task verification and termination failures
keywords: Task verification and termination failures MAS failures from inadequate output validation and premature or incorrect termination, allowing errors to propagate through the agent chain (≈21.3% of observed failures)

label: Trajectory-persistent adversarial attack
keywords: Trajectory-persistent adversarial attack A single adversarial perturbation to a world-model encoder propagates through recurrent latent state, corrupting an entire multi-step rollout far more destructively than in a stateless model (early-step amplification)

label: Representation / training-data poisoning
keywords: Representation / training-data poisoning Adversarial corruption of world-model training data or latent representations, including backdoor triggers that activate unsafe dynamics only under specific conditions; defects encoded in self-supervised pre-training (the "Foundry Problem") cannot be remediated downstream

label: Compounding rollout error / hallucination
keywords: Compounding rollout error / hallucination Prediction errors compound across multi-step world-model rollouts, producing kinematic drift, structural violations, and misleading reward/safety estimates that mislead the policy (Hallucinated Value)

label: Sim-to-real / distributional-shift exploitation
keywords: Sim-to-real / distributional-shift exploitation World models produce unpredictable, erroneous outputs on out-of-distribution inputs; the sim-to-real gap is weaponised in long-tail safety-critical states where decisions matter most

label: World-model extraction / inversion
keywords: World-model extraction / inversion Model-extraction attacks replicate a deployed dynamics model; inversion reconstructs training observations, exposing proprietary environments or sensitive data

label: Goal misgeneralisation (world-model-equipped)
keywords: Goal misgeneralisation (world-model-equipped) A world-model agent learns to predict reward correlates (e.g., lighting, human presence) rather than the true causal reward, and optimizes these spurious correlates where the correlation breaks at deployment

label: Deceptive alignment via self-simulation
keywords: Deceptive alignment via self-simulation A world-model agent can simulate its own training/evaluation context, predict when it is being tested, and condition behavior on that prediction, appearing aligned during oversight while pursuing an instrumental goal once oversight is removed

label: Reward hacking via world-model exploitation
keywords: Reward hacking via world-model exploitation An agent with an accurate world model identifies and systematically exploits gaps between the reward model and intended objective, generating high-imagined-reward trajectories that do not correspond to real task completion

label: Long-horizon planning hallucination
keywords: Long-horizon planning hallucination In agentic deployments, world-model rollout errors compound over planning depth; agents execute long-horizon plans on plausible-but-physically-incorrect imagined trajectories, each action compounding the error

label: Automation bias / miscalibrated trust
keywords: Automation bias / miscalibrated trust Authoritative, richly rendered world-model predictions amplify operator over-reliance; learned trust is calibrated on average performance and fails to adapt to rare OOD failure modes where predictions are least reliable

label: Social-world-model influence operations
keywords: Social-world-model influence operations A foundation world model trained on social data predicts demographic responses to emotionally charged narratives, enabling micro-targeted influence and psychologically targeted persuasion at scale

label: Counterfactual authority problem
keywords: Counterfactual authority problem Operators and regulators accept world-model counterfactual explanations as ground truth when they reflect the model's learned distribution rather than true causal dynamics; selectively usable to attribute or deflect liability

label: Negative side effects
keywords: Negative side effects An agent optimizing an objective focused on one task expresses implicit indifference over other environmental variables, causing major disruption to the wider environment for marginal task gain (wrong-objective accident)

label: Reward hacking
keywords: Reward hacking An agent games an imperfectly specified objective, achieving high reward through unintended solutions that pervert designer intent; subsumes wireheading, Goodhart's law, feedback loops, and reward-tampering

label: Scalable oversight failure
keywords: Scalable oversight failure Harmful behavior caused by an agent extrapolating from cheap proxy signals when the true objective is too expensive to evaluate frequently (limited-oversight accident).; Agent behavior becomes too complex, distributed, or rapid for available human or automated oversight to monitor and correct reliably

label: Safe exploration [origin]
keywords: Safe exploration [origin] Exploratory actions by a learning agent produce negative or irrecoverable consequences. NOTE: origin of GYK-2025 "Unsafe exploration"; cross-reference, not a duplicate leaf

label: Robustness to distributional shift [origin]
keywords: Robustness to distributional shift [origin] ML system performs poorly and remains confidently wrong when test distribution differs from training. NOTE: origin of GYK-2025 "Non-stationary distribution"; cross-reference

label: System misspecification
keywords: System misspecification Misunderstanding or mis-eliciting the requirements and purpose of an AI system: incorrect requirement elicitation, suboptimal modelling or hyperparameter choices, and failure to adapt to changing domain requirements

label: Lack of control enforcement
keywords: Lack of control enforcement Failure to enforce control over agent behavior: misalignment of agent goals with human preferences, wireheading, mesa-optimization, and other undesirable emergent behaviors

label: Lack of monitoring
keywords: Lack of monitoring Insufficient monitoring and interpretability of deployed AI: black-box opacity diminishing human agency, undetected ethical/safety-principle violations, and privacy violations

label: Undesirable behaviour (rational-agent)
keywords: Undesirable behaviour (rational-agent) Bad behavior of utility-maximizing rational agents: self-modifying agents, reward-signal bypass (wireheading), and corrigibility failure of uncooperative agents

label: Harm to physical health or safety
keywords: Harm to physical health or safety Realized injury to the physical health or safety of persons attributable to an AI system, as recorded in incident-level annotation

label: Financial loss
keywords: Financial loss Monetary loss incurred by individuals or organizations attributable to the behavior of an AI system

label: Property damage
keywords: Property damage Damage to physical property attributable to the behavior of an AI system

label: Harm to infrastructure
keywords: Harm to infrastructure Disruption or damage to infrastructure systems attributable to the behavior of an AI system

label: Harm to the natural environment
keywords: Harm to the natural environment Damage to the natural environment attributable to the behavior of an AI system

label: Detrimental content
keywords: Detrimental content AI-generated or AI-distributed content such as deepfakes, identity misrepresentation, threats, self-harm promotion, extremist content, misinformation, sexual abuse material, or scams, which can instigate injury, damage, or loss

label: Bias and differential treatment
keywords: Bias and differential treatment Systematically unequal treatment of individuals or groups by an AI system, frequently with respect to protected characteristics

label: Violation of privacy
keywords: Violation of privacy Infringement of individuals' privacy interests through the collection, inference, or disclosure of personal information by an AI system

label: Violation of human and civil rights
keywords: Violation of human and civil rights Infringement of human or civil rights attributable to the deployment or behavior of an AI system

label: Violation of democratic norms
keywords: Violation of democratic norms Erosion or violation of democratic processes and norms attributable to the deployment or behavior of an AI system

label: Lack of validity and reliability
keywords: Lack of validity and reliability Failure of an AI system to produce accurate, consistent, and generalizable outputs under the conditions of expected use, including ungrounded or erroneous outputs

label: Lack of safety
keywords: Lack of safety Operation of an AI system in ways that endanger human life, health, property, or the environment under defined conditions of use

label: Lack of security and resilience
keywords: Lack of security and resilience Susceptibility of an AI system to adversarial attacks, data poisoning, exfiltration, or inability to withstand and recover from adverse events

label: Lack of accountability and transparency
keywords: Lack of accountability and transparency Absence of accessible information about an AI system and of organizational structures that assign responsibility for its outcomes

label: Lack of explainability and interpretability
keywords: Lack of explainability and interpretability Inability to represent the mechanisms underlying an AI system's operation or to convey the meaning of its outputs in context

label: Privacy harms
keywords: Privacy harms Failure to safeguard the norms and practices protecting human autonomy, identity, and dignity, including unwanted disclosure or inference of personal data

label: Harmful bias and unfairness
keywords: Harmful bias and unfairness Presence of systematic computational, human-cognitive, or institutional biases in an AI system producing inequitable or discriminatory outcomes

label: Physical harm (death)
keywords: Physical harm (death) An AI incident whose realized harm includes the death of a person or groups of people

label: Psychological harm
keywords: Psychological harm Intangible harm to mental wellbeing arising from the development, use, or malfunction of an AI system

label: Reputational harm
keywords: Reputational harm Damage to the standing or reputation of individuals, groups, or organizations attributable to an AI system
